commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.repair_reason: structured ClawSweeper needs-human verdict with repairable P-severity findings (sha=d971c9b929bd21a1421d09675c52cbf1c0c30de8)
commands.review_summary:
Adds an opt-in `cron.minInterval` config setting with schema/docs/help wiring and service/gateway enforcement for recurring `every` and `cron` job creation or schedule edits.

PR surface: Source +145, Tests +187, Docs +9. Total +341 across 15 files.

Reproducibility: yes. for the PR defect: the source shows only six sampled fires, and a Croner 10.0.1 probe shows a later 1-day burst that the first six fires would miss. This is not a current-main user bug because the config option is new in this PR.
commands.review_followup: - [P2] A maintainer should decide whether the new config option promises exact cron-expression enforcement or a bounded sample before an automated repair tries to lock in semantics.

target.title: feat(cron): configurable minimum interval guardrail for recurring jobs
target.body:
## Summary

Adds an opt-in `cron.minInterval` setting that enforces a **minimum interval between fires for recurring cron jobs**. Creating or editing an `every`/`cron` job whose tightest interval falls below the configured floor is rejected with a clear error.

Motivation: enforcing a minimum cron frequency is a guardrail that belongs in an exposed OpenClaw setting rather than in each downstream backend. This mirrors the existing `cron.maxConcurrentRuns` operator guardrail and gives one enforcement point that covers every creation path (agent tool, CLI, gateway RPC).

## What changed

- **Config surface**: new `cron.minInterval` (`CronConfig` type, zod schema + duration validation, help text, label, docs). Accepts a duration string (`30s`, `5m`, `1h`) or a number of milliseconds; bare numbers are milliseconds. Default `0`/unset = no limit.
- **Resolver**: `resolveCronMinIntervalMs()` in `src/config/cron-limits.ts`, directly paralleling `resolveCronMaxConcurrentRuns()`.
- **Enforcement**: `assertScheduleMeetsMinInterval()` in the cron service, called from `createJob` (add) and from `update` when the schedule changes.
  - `every` jobs: `everyMs` compared to the floor.
  - `cron` expressions: judged by the **smallest gap** across a few sampled consecutive fires, so irregular bursts like `0,1 * * * *` are caught (croner supports seconds, so `* * * * * *` = 1s).
  - One-shot `at` jobs are exempt; a floor of `0` disables the guardrail.
- **Scope decisions** (compatibility-safe): default off so existing fast jobs keep working; updates only re-validate when the schedule itself changes, so unrelated edits to a pre-existing fast job are not blocked; manual/forced runs are unaffected.
- **Surfacing**: the rejection maps to `INVALID_REQUEST` in the gateway cron handler, and the cron tool description tells the agent to increase the interval instead of retrying.

## Config example

```json5
{
  cron: {
    minInterval: "5m", // reject recurring jobs that fire more often than every 5 minutes
  },
}
```

Error shape: `cron schedule fires every 30s, below the minimum interval of 5m`.

## Verification

Run locally (clean):

- `oxfmt --check` on all touched files — clean
- `oxlint` on touched files — clean
- `tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json` and `-p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json` — exit 0
- `check-import-cycles` — 0 runtime value cycles
- Vitest (targeted):
  - `src/config/cron-limits.test.ts`, `src/config/zod-schema.cron-min-interval.test.ts`
  - `src/cron/service/jobs.min-interval.test.ts` (every/cron/at, boundary, irregular-gap, create-path)
  - Regression: `src/cron/service.jobs.test.ts`, `jobs.apply-patch.test.ts`, `ops.test.ts`, `server-cron.test.ts`, `server.cron.test.ts`, `schema.help.quality.test.ts`, `schema.hints.test.ts`, `validation.allowed-values.test.ts`, `defaults.test.ts`, `tools-invoke-http.cron-regression.test.ts`, `cron-tool.test.ts` — all green

Broader changed-gate / Crabbox proof not yet run (draft).

## Release note

feat(cron): add `cron.minInterval` to set a minimum interval for recurring cron jobs as a guardrail against accidental high-frequency schedules. Thanks @amknight (requested by Tom Wood).
target.author: amknight
